results.possibleIdentity: Ronald Mallory (June 17, 1932 – July 7, 2021) was an American kinetic artist and architect primarily active from the mid-1960s through the late 20th century. He is best known for his pioneering 'mercury pictures'—kinetic sculptures featuring liquid mercury trapped between plexiglass plates, often driven by slow-turning motors to create fluid, shifting compositions. Born in Philadelphia, Mallory studied architecture at the University of Florida and worked in Brazil with modernist masters Oscar Niemeyer and Roberto Burle Marx, the latter of whom encouraged him to focus on fine art. After living in Paris for several years and exhibiting at Galerie Iris Clert in 1961, he returned to New York City where he shared a studio with the Belgian kinetic artist Pol Bury. Mallory was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group and was a prominent figure in the New York art scene of the late 1960s. His work was featured in a major solo exhibition at the Stable Gallery in 1966 and is included in the permanent collections of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art (SFMOMA). Later in his career, he explored other mediums including digital art and holograms.

results.possibleIdentity: Barbara Gormley was an administrator and editor associated with the New York experimental art and technology scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. She served as an early staff member and administrator for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), the influential non-profit organization founded in 1966 by Billy Klüver, Robert Rauschenberg, Robert Whitman, and Fred Waldhauer to facilitate collaborations between artists and engineers. In her capacity at E.A.T., she was responsible for organizing the organization's records and activities, leading to her role as the compiler of 'Experiments in Art and Technology: A Bibliography of the Organization and its Activities,' published by the organization in 1971. In addition to her administrative work, Gormley was a poet and a member of the Eventorium group, a circle of poets and artists based on Manhattan's Upper West Side. Her poetry was featured in the group's magazine, 'Eventorium Muse' (active 1964–1967), alongside contributors such as Michael Benedikt and Russell Edson.

results.possibleIdentity: David Rothenberg (d. 2018) was a musical and mathematical theorist, researcher, and artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his work in pattern perception and his collaboration with synthesizer pioneer Robert Moog. In the mid-1960s, Rothenberg designed a specialized keyboard for an analog synthesizer with a 31-tone octave (instead of the traditional 12-tone octave), which Moog was hired to build. This project was funded by a grant from the Air Force Office of Scientific Research and was related to his work on pattern recognition. Rothenberg's interdisciplinary work bridging music, mathematics, and engineering aligns with the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1970, he was a featured speaker at the 'Computers and the Arts' seminar held at Simon Fraser University, alongside other notable early computer art pioneers. His widow, Suhasini Sankaran, donated his specialized 31-tone keyboard and instrument records to the Cornell Center for Historical Instruments in 2022.

David Rothenberg (b. 1933) is a prominent New York-based theatre producer, director, and activist who was highly active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He began his career as a theatrical press agent on Broadway, working with celebrities such as Elizabeth Taylor, Richard Burton, and Alvin Ailey. In 1967, he produced the groundbreaking and controversial Off-Broadway play 'Fortune and Men's Eyes', which dramatized the brutal realities of prison life and homosexuality behind bars. The play's success and the subsequent public response led him to found the Fortune Society in 1967, a non-profit organization focused on prison reform and prisoner reentry services. Throughout the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was a central figure in the Greenwich Village arts scene, producing socially conscious works like the anti-war play 'Viet Rock' (1966).

results.possibleIdentity: Harley Jones was a prominent filmmaker, editor, and educator active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He was the first lecturer in cinematography at the Newport College of Art (later part of the University of Wales) when the film school was founded in 1966. He had a background as a film editor for the BBC and worked closely with John Grierson, the father of the British documentary movement, on the programme 'This Wonderful World.' Jones was known for creating experimental and 'impressionistic' films that blended documentary styles with animation and intermedia techniques. His notable works from this period include 'Resurrection' (1966), an abstract homage to steam locomotives, and 'After Many a Summer' (1968), which documented the redevelopment of the Tiger Bay community in Cardiff. His involvement in the Newport College of Art placed him at the center of a highly experimental environment that promoted 'intermedia presentation,' a concept closely aligned with the mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He also served on panels for the Arts Council of Great Britain's Film Committee in the late 1970s.

Harley Jones is a New Zealand-born accordionist and musical entrepreneur who was active in the late 1960s. Born in 1953, he rose to prominence as a child prodigy, winning the New Zealand Accordion Championship at age 14 and representing his country at the Coupe Mondiale International Accordion Solo Competition in New York in 1969. While primarily a musician and performer, his career expanded into performance recording and digital media; in 1996, he founded the 'Accordions Worldwide' group of websites. During the 1970s, he performed an extensive repertoire ranging from classical transcriptions to original compositions and recorded L.P. albums.

results.possibleIdentity: Patricia Hermine Sloane (1934–2001), commonly known as Pat Sloane, was an American painter, filmmaker, critic, and professor of Fine Arts. She was active in the New York art scene starting in the late 1950s and was a member of the 10th Street Galleries artist cooperative, specifically the Camino Gallery, alongside artists like Alice Neel and Elaine de Kooning. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, her work moved from Abstract Expressionism toward geometric art. She was also a filmmaker, producing the non-narrative film "A Knee Ad" (1970), which she described as a "cinematic statement on motion." Sloane was an influential figure in the study of color theory, authoring "Color: Basic Principles and New Directions" (1968) and "The Visual Nature of Color" (1989), and her 1972 NYU PhD dissertation focused on a critique of 19th and 20th-century color theory. Her association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement is reflected in historical accounts of the intersection of art and modern technology, where she is frequently cited for her theoretical work on the scientific nature of color. She was also a prominent voice in the feminist art movement, chairing panels on women artists at the College Art Association in 1973. Sloane taught at the New York City Technical College of the City University of New York for many years and was a Guggenheim Fellow in 1974.
